literal: being, existing; present participle of √as 'to be'
invariant_definition: Being or reality, but in technical contexts more than the bare copula 'is'. *Sat* marks what stands up under ontological and causal analysis. The term therefore appears in several linked debates: whether only the ultimate is *sat* in the strict sense or whether dependent realities are also genuinely *sat*; whether effects arise from what already is, as *sat-kārya-vāda* argues, or from prior non-manifestation; and how *sat* differs from both *asat* and, in Advaita, *mithyā*. Upaniṣadic usage gives the term high metaphysical weight, especially in *sad eva somya idam agra āsīt*; later schools then ask what exactly counts as 'what is'. For Advaita, *sat* in the strict sense belongs only to *brahman*. For realist Vedāntas and Sāṃkhya, dependent entities are also real, though not equally independent. A translation that treats *sat* as mere existence misses this layered use in causality, ontology, and soteriology.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, *sat* in the strict sense belongs only to unsublated *brahman*, the *turīya* that is *prapañcopaśama* and *advaita* [[MāṇḍU 7](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/upaniṣad.7)]. What appears in *vyavahāra* cannot be sheer *asat*, but neither is it *sat* as Brahman is; hence *avidyā* and its projections are said to be neither *sat* nor *asat* but *anirvacanīya* [[BS 1.1](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]. The culminant standpoint accordingly denies origination, bondage, striving, and release at the level of *paramārthatā*, because only non-dual reality is strictly what is [[GK 2.32](cite://sankara/mandukya-bhasya/2.32)].

For Viśiṣṭādvaita, *sat* is among the Vedāntic designators of Nārāyaṇa, the *paratattva* who is inner controller and causal ground of the entire *cid-acid-prapañca* [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Because the cosmos of selves and matter stands to him through *śarīra-śarīrin*-sambandha and *sāmānādhikaraṇya*, their dependence does not cancel their reality; it specifies the way the real is qualified [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. In Vedānta Deśika's scholastic gloss, *sat* can therefore be parsed normatively as what is *prāmāṇika* and *mumukṣor upādeya*, so ontology and salvific uptake remain internally linked [[SAS ad 1.2b](cite://vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2b)] [[SAS ad 1.2c](cite://vedanta-desika/sarvartha-siddhi/ad 1.2c)].

In Tattva-vāda, the manifold of fivefold difference is itself *satya* and *anādi*; plurality is not an epistemic defect but the structure of the real under Viṣṇu's sovereignty [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Accordingly the school refuses any fifth ontological category distinct from *bhāva* and *abhāva*, holding that neither scripture nor reason licenses an *anirvacanīya* middle [[Tattvodyota](cite://madhva/tattvodyota/Scripture Teaches a Category Beyond Being and Non-Being)].
primary_loci: Tattvodyota, "No Scripture Teaches a Category Beyond Being and Non-Being"

citations.cite: cite://madhva/tattvodyota/Scripture Teaches a Category Beyond Being and Non-Being

per_school.definition: In the proto-Bhedābheda represented by Bhartṛprapañca, Brahman's being is not exhausted by an undifferentiated absolute: the unmanifest becomes articulated as *nāma-rūpa*, and that manifestation is taken as real rather than merely pedagogical [[BṛU 1.4.7](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.7)]. The same Brahman is taught as both *mūrta* and *amūrta*; the two registers are held together rather than hierarchized away, so *sat* covers differentiated and undifferentiated modes within a single ontological field [[BṛU 2.3.1, 2.3.6](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.3)].

In Acintya-Bhedābheda, Bhagavān is the *parama-svarūpa* of all, while the plurality of *jīvas* stands to him through real difference and non-difference grounded in his *svābhāvika-acintya-śakti* [[PS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)] [[PS §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)]. The school's use of *sat* is accordingly not a reduction to bare existence but a theologically saturated reality-claim: Bhagavān is the highest intrinsic reality, and dependent beings are real as ray-like manifestations rather than illusory overlays [[PS §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)] [[PS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)].

In Nimbārka's Dvaitādvaita, the *jīva* is *harer adhīna*, atomic, and genuinely distinct in each body, while Brahman is the faultless and infinitely auspicious Kṛṣṇa [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)] [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)]. The reality denoted by *sat* is thus neither sheer identity nor sheer separation: dependent selves and the supreme Brahman are both real, but their reality is ordered by dependence on the supreme [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)] [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)].

For Śuddhādvaita, Brahman pervades as *asti-bhāti-priya* in the form of *sat-cid-ānanda*, and the world of name and form is an effect-state whose reality rests in that very Brahman [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. Because the cause is not displaced by its manifestation, *sat* is not reserved for a worldless absolute; it names Brahman precisely as the real ground that remains present through manifest plurality [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)].

For Mādhyamaka, discourse proceeds by the two truths: *lokasaṃvṛti-satya* and *paramārtha-satya* [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)]. What is ultimate is not a positively self-standing *sat* behind appearances, but the emptiness of whatever arises dependently; hence *paramārtha* is taught only through convention and resolved as *pratītyasamutpāda*-understood-as-*śūnyatā* [[MMK 24.10](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.10)] [[MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)].

In the Yogācāra-epistemological register represented by Dharmakīrti, *sat* is explicitly split into *paramārthasat* and *saṃvṛtisat* [[PV 2.3](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3)]. What is *paramārthasat* is whatever is *arthakriyāsamartha*; the conventionally real is the conceptual or generality-structured 'other,' so reality is sorted by efficacy rather than by naive objecthood [[PV 2.3](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.3)].

translator_note: Avoid treating *sat* as mere existential vocabulary. English 'being' and 'existence' encourage a thin, quantificational reading, whereas Sanskrit usage often marks robust reality, causal persistence, and resistance to sublation. Also keep *sat* distinct from *satya* ('true') and from the Advaita contrast-term *mithyā*.
